--- SOURCE 2: Mastering Narrative Technique - Mary Kole Editorial ---
URL: https://www.marykole.com/narrative-technique

SUMMARY:
Narrative technique refers to the tools a writer uses to tell a story, including writing imagery, objective correlative, point of view, interiority, and other craft elements. As a writer, you should understand common narrative techniques so that you may hone your writing craft and create engaging, well-told stories from your book idea. Narrative techniques are essential for creating engaging, well-crafted stories. Understanding the most popular contemporary narrative techniques, when to use them, and how to avoid common pitfalls such as unclear narrative voice and excessive jumping around in chronology, will help writers to create engaging, well-written storytelling. Remember, narrative technique is what sets your unique writing style apart from others, so don’t hesitate to explore by freewriting and reading like a writer as well.

![Mary Kole Editorial](//images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/6400c3d24deed10a9ac9ec44/fb2dd393-33b2-4687-8cb8-bfc4aed1e857/MKE+Logo+-+Cream.png?format=1500w)
![Mary Kole Editorial](//images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/6400c3d24deed10a9ac9ec44/fb2dd393-33b2-4687-8cb8-bfc4aed1e857/MKE+Logo+-+Cream.png?format=1500w)

# Mastering Narrative Technique

![](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/6400c3d24deed10a9ac9ec44/4f061b8a-777a-42d5-9e03-f47bd526218a/Kole+Large+Headshot+%282%29.jpg)

By Mary Kole

Mary Kole is a former literary agent, freelance editor, writing teacher, author of *Writing Irresistible Kidlit*, and IP developer for major publishers, with over a decade in the publishing industry.

Aspiring writers often struggle with mastering narrative technique. Narrative technique, story structure, or the way a story is told, is key to engaging readers and moving them emotionally over the course of a [novel](https://marykole.com/novel-writing) or [memoir](https://marykole.com/memoir-writing). In this guide, I’ll discuss the most common and popular contemporary narrative techniques, how to use narrative technique effectively, and common mistakes writers make when adding more advanced considerations to their [writing tools](https://marykole.com/writer-tools).

## **What is Narrative Technique?**

Narrative technique refers to the tools a writer uses to tell a story, including [writing imagery](https://kidlit.com/writing-imagery), [objective correlative](https://marykole.com/objective-correlative), [point of view](https://kidlit.com/pov), [interiority](https://kidlit.com/interiority), and other craft elements. From who narrates the story, how the story is told ([flashback](https://kidlit.com/flashback), [flashforward](https://marykole.com/flashforward), non-linear narration, stream-of-consciousness), to [writing style](https://marykole.com/writing-style) choices like syntax, [word choice](https://marykole.com/word-choice), and [writing voice](https://kidlit.com/writing-voice). Narrative technique is what sets apart one writer's unique style from someone else’s. As a writer, you should understand common narrative techniques so that you may hone your [writing craft](https://kidlit.com/writing-craft) and create engaging, well-told stories from your [book idea](https://kidlit.com/how-to-make-a-story-interesting).

![](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/6400c3d24deed10a9ac9ec44/32d7e3c6-0c97-4d00-b663-992459d3f09c/narrative+technique.png)

## **Contemporary Narrative Technique**

In contemporary literature, common narrative techniques include [first-person point of view](https://marykole.com/first-person-point-of-view), third-person limited POV, and third-person omniscient, though this one is more difficult to pull off well and intentionally. First-person point of view is written from the perspective of "I," placing the reader inside the narrative voice's head through deep perspective and interiority. Third-person limited describes a story from an outsider's perspective but is told through the eyes of one character, without “head-hopping” into the larger cast. Lastly, third-person omniscient is told from an external perspective but shares the thoughts and feelings of various characters. [Authorial voice](https://kidlit.com/authorial-voice/), or that of an outside narrator, can sometimes be applied to the [storytelling](https://kidlit.com/storytelling).

## **The Importance of Narrative Technique**

Narrative techniques are essential for creating engaging, well-crafted stories. By using various techniques, writers create interesting opportunities for [plotting](https://kidlit.com/plotting), [pacing](https://kidlit.com/story-pacing), and [character development](https://goodstorycompany.com/blog/character-development). Consider the [unreliable narrator](https://marykole.com/unreliable-narrator), a narrative technique used to experiment with theme and subvert the text's meaning, existing in one character’s experience, rather than objective reality, or toying with dramatic irony. This can create [suspense](https://kidlit.com/writing-suspense) and intrigue for a reader, especially in a [thriller](https://goodstorycompany.com/thriller-tropes) or [mystery](https://goodstorycompany.com/mystery-tropes) story.

## **Narrative Technique Mistakes to Avoid**

One common mistake made by novice writers is failing to maintain a clear narrative voice. A confused narrative voice can make it difficult for a reader to understand the story and connect with the characters emotionally. Another mistake occurs in the use of flashbacks or non-linear narrative. You don’t always want to use chronological narration because this can be considered too straightforward, but you also want to put focus on present moment narrative, rather than past. When used excessively, flashbacks can disrupt the story's flow and confuse readers. Instead, use flashbacks strategically, providing them only when necessary for character development or to clarify story moments through [backstory](https://kidlit.com/backstory). One of the main purposes of any narrative technique is to find the right balance of action and information, and make sure that you [show, don’t tell](https://goodstorycompany.com/blog/show-dont-tell) the events of your story, since readers want to experience your plot firsthand.

Mastering various narrative technique options is a vital part of the writing craft. Understanding the most popular contemporary narrative techniques, when to use them, and how to avoid common pitfalls such as unclear narrative voice and excessive jumping around in chronology, will help writers to create engaging, well-written storytelling. Write intentionally to ensure that your story idea is told in the most effective and meaningful way possible, with strong [character arcs](https://www.goodstorycompany.com/blog/character-arc) and [plot arcs](https://marykole.com/plot-arc), captivating readers from beginning to end. Remember, narrative technique is what sets your unique writing style apart from others, so don’t hesitate to explore by [freewriting](https://goodstorycompany.com/freewriting) and [reading like a writer](https://www.goodstorycompany.com/blog/reading-like-a-writer) as well. [Brainstorming](https://marykole.com/brainstorming) and writing inspiration are all around us.

![](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/6400c3d24deed10a9ac9ec44/9deb166a-c595-43d5-88bd-e5d105de5768/Rectangle+11.png)
![](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/6400c3d24deed10a9ac9ec44/69c697a8-1e9c-42e6-82a7-b1cf28c87491/pb+mke.png)

Click [here](https://www.goodstorycompany.com/writing-interiority) to purchase *Writing Interiority: Crafting Irresistible Characters,* my book on interiority and character creation. Explore your protagonist’s thoughts, feelings, reactions and interpretations, expectations, and inner struggles to create a rich, immersive experience. This guide will empower you to create characters who live and breathe on the page, fostering an unbreakable bond with your audience.

URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_narrative_techniques

SUMMARY:
A **narrative technique** (also, in fiction, a **fictional device**) is any of several storytelling methods the creator of a story uses, thus effectively relaying information to the audience or making the story more complete, complex, or engaging. | Setting "Setting (narrative)") as a form of symbolism or allegory | The setting is both the time and geographic location within a narrative or within a work of fiction; sometimes, storytellers use the setting as a way to represent deeper ideas, reflect characters' emotions, or encourage the audience to make certain connections that add complexity to how the story may be interpreted. | First-person narration | A text presented from the point of view of a character, especially the protagonist, as if the character is telling the story themselves.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

List of methods used to convey information in a narrative

A **narrative technique** (also, in [fiction](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fiction "Fiction"), a **fictional device**) is any of several [storytelling](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Storytelling "Storytelling") methods the creator of a [story](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Narrative "Narrative") uses,[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_narrative_techniques#cite_note-1) thus effectively relaying information to the audience or making the story more complete, complex, or engaging. Some scholars also call such a technique a **narrative mode**, though this term can also more narrowly refer to the particular technique of [using a commentary](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Narration "Narration") to deliver a story. Other possible synonyms within written narratives are **literary technique** or **literary device**, though these can also broadly refer to non-narrative writing strategies, as might be used in academic or essay writing, as well as [poetic devices](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Poetic_devices "Poetic devices") such as [assonance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Assonance "Assonance"), [metre](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metre_(poetry) "Metre (poetry)"), or [rhyme scheme](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rhyme_scheme "Rhyme scheme"). Furthermore, narrative techniques are distinguished from [narrative elements](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Narrative#Basic_elements "Narrative"), which exist inherently in all works of narrative, rather than being merely optional strategies.

Setting
-------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=List_of_narrative_techniques&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Setting")]

| Name | Definition | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [Setting](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Setting_(narrative) "Setting (narrative)") as a form of [symbolism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Artistic_symbol "Artistic symbol") or [allegory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Allegory "Allegory") | The setting is both the time and geographic location within a narrative or within a work of fiction; sometimes, storytellers use the setting as a way to represent deeper ideas, reflect characters' emotions, or encourage the audience to make certain connections that add complexity to how the story may be interpreted. | The novel _[Ulysses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ulysses\_(novel) "Ulysses (novel)")_ by James Joyce is set in [Dublin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dublin "Dublin"), [Ireland](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"), over the course of a single day, 16 June 1904. The novel spans from [Sandycove](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sandycove "Sandycove") in the South to [Howth Head](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Howth_Head "Howth Head") in the North, with detailed descriptions of [Dublin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dublin "Dublin") locations that parallel those in [Homer's](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Homer "Homer")_[Odyssey](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Odyssey "Odyssey")_. [Leopold Bloom](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leopold_Bloom "Leopold Bloom")'s home at [7 Eccles Street](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/7_Eccles_Street "7 Eccles Street") serves as a modern-day [Ithaca](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Homer%27s_Ithaca "Homer's Ithaca"), while other landmarks represent various episodes of the Odyssey. ِAlso, in [Tayeb Salih](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tayeb_Salih "Tayeb Salih")'s _[Season of Migration to the North](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Season\_of\_Migration\_to\_the\_North "Season of Migration to the North")_ (1966), the setting is crucial. The story begins in the Sudanese village of Wad Hamed before shifting to [London, England](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/London "London"). This contrast between the two locations highlights the protagonist Mustafa Saeed's struggle with cultural, social, and psychological challenges as he moves between his homeland and the West. Wad Hamed symbolizes tradition and rural life, while London represents modernity and colonial influence. Salih skillfully employs setting to explore themes of identity, cultural clash, and colonialism's enduring impact.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_narrative_techniques#cite_note-2) |

Plots
-----

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=List_of_narrative_techniques&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Plots")]

| Name | Definition | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [Backstory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Backstory "Backstory") | Story that precedes events in the story being told—past events or background that add meaning to current circumstances | Though _[The Lord of the Rings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The\_Lord\_of\_the\_Rings "The Lord of the Rings")_ trilogy takes place in a relatively short period towards the end of the 3021-year Third Age, the narration gives glimpses of the mythological and historical events which took place earlier in the Third age leading up to the action in the novel, and in the First and Second Age. |
| [Chekhov's gun](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chekhov%27s_gun "Chekhov's gun") | A dramatic principle that requires every element in a narrative to be substantive, with anything redundant or arbitrary removed. | "Remove everything that has no relevance to the story. If you say in the first chapter that there is a rifle hanging on the wall, in the second or third chapter it absolutely must go off. If it's not going to be fired, it shouldn't be hanging there." — Anton Chekhov |
| [Cliffhanger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cliffhanger "Cliffhanger") | The narrative ends unresolved, to draw the audience back to a future episode for the resolution. | Almost every episode of TV shows like _[Dexter](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dexter\_(TV\_series) "Dexter (TV series)")_ and _[Breaking Bad](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Breaking\_Bad "Breaking Bad")_[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_narrative_techniques#cite_note-3) ends with one of the characters in a predicament (about to be caught by thugs, about to be exposed by the authorities, or a family member or a friend finds out the main character's dirty secret). |
| [Eucatastrophe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eucatastrophe "Eucatastrophe") | Coined by [J. R. R. Tolkien](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J._R._R._Tolkien "J. R. R. Tolkien"), a climactic event through which the protagonist appears to be facing a catastrophic change. However, this change does not materialize and the protagonist finds themselves benefiting from the climactic event; contrast [peripety](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peripety "Peripety")/_peripateia_. | At the end of _The Lord of the Rings_, Gollum forcibly takes away the Ring from Frodo, suggesting that [Sauron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sauron "Sauron") would eventually take over Middle Earth. However, Gollum celebrates too eagerly and clumsily falls into the lava, whereby the ring is destroyed and with it Sauron's power. In a way, Gollum does what Frodo and the Fellowship of the Ring intended to do through the whole plot of the trilogy, which was to throw the ring into the lake of fire in the heart of Mount Doom. |
| [Flashback](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flashback_(narrative) "Flashback (narrative)") (or analepsis) | Alteration of time sequences, taking characters back to the beginning of the tale, for instance | The story of "[The Three Apples](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Three_Apples "The Three Apples")" in the _[Arabian Nights](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arabian\_Nights "Arabian Nights")_ tales begins with the discovery of a young woman's dead body. After the murderer later reveals himself, he narrates his reasons for the murder as a flashback of events leading up to the discovery of her dead body at the beginning of the story. |
| [Flashforward](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flashforward "Flashforward") (or prolepsis) | A scene that temporarily jumps the narrative forward in time. A flashforward often represents events expected, projected, or imagined to occur in the future. They may also reveal significant parts of the story that have not yet occurred, but soon will in greater detail. | Occurs in _[A Christmas Carol](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/A\_Christmas\_Carol "A Christmas Carol")_ when Mr. Scrooge visits the ghost of the future. It is also frequent in the later seasons of the television series [_Lost_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lost_(2004_TV_series) "Lost (2004 TV series)"). |
| [Foreshadowing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Foreshadowing "Foreshadowing") | Implicit yet intentional efforts of an author to suggest events that have yet to take place in the process of narration. See also [repetitive designation](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Repetitive_designation&action=edit&redlink=1 "Repetitive designation (page does not exist)") and [Chekhov's gun](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chekhov%27s_gun "Chekhov's gun") | A narration might begin with a male character who has to break up a schoolyard fight among some boys who are vying for the attention of a girl, which was introduced to foreshadow the events leading to a dinner time squabble between the character and his twin brother over a woman, whom both are courting at the same time. |
| [Frame story](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frame_story "Frame story"), or a [story within a story](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Story_within_a_story "Story within a story") | A main story that hatches a framing device, a story that is "framed" in the main story. | Early examples include _[Panchatantra](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Panchatantra "Panchatantra")_, _[Kalila and Dimna](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kalila\_and\_Dimna "Kalila and Dimna")_, _[Arabian Nights](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The\_Book\_of\_One\_Thousand\_and\_One\_Nights "The Book of One Thousand and One Nights")_, and _[The Decameron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The\_Decameron "The Decameron")_. More modern examples are [Brian Jacques](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brian_Jacques "Brian Jacques")'s 1999 _[The Legend of Luke](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The\_Legend\_of\_Luke "The Legend of Luke")_, [Ramsay Wood](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ramsay_Wood "Ramsay Wood")'s 2011 _[Kalila and Dimna](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kalila\_and\_Dimna "Kalila and Dimna")_ update, subtitled _Fables of Conflict and Intrigue_ and [Sophia de Mello Breyner Andresen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sophia_de_Mello_Breyner_Andresen "Sophia de Mello Breyner Andresen")'s 1964 _The Knight of Denmark_ (O cavaleiro da Dinamarca). |
| _[In medias res](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/In\_medias\_res "In medias res")_ | Beginning the story in the middle of a sequence of events. A specific form of narrative hook. | This is used in epic poems, for example, where it is a mandatory form to be adopted. Luís de Camões' _The Lusiads_ or the _Iliad_ and the _Odyssey_ of Homer are prime examples. The latter work begins with the return of Odysseus to his home of Ithaca and then in flashbacks tells of his ten years of wandering following the Trojan War. _The Lusiads_ starts in the middle of the sea voyage to India and contextualizes the beginning of said journey as well as Portugal's history when the master of the ship tells an African king about it. |
| [Narrative hook](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Narrative_hook "Narrative hook") | Story opening that "hooks" readers' attention so they will keep reading | Any non-fiction book is often introduced with an interesting factoid. |
| [MacGuffin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MacGuffin "MacGuffin") | Object required to initiate the plot or motivation of the characters, but having little significance by itself | "Heart of the Ocean" necklace in James Cameron's 1997 Titanic, which essentially serves as an object to cause Rose to tell her story. |
| [Ochi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ochi "Ochi") | A sudden interruption of the wordplay flow indicating the end of a [rakugo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rakugo "Rakugo") or a kobanashi. | A Rakugo is a Japanese verbal entertainment usually lasting 30 minutes which ends with a surprise punch line, a narrative stunt known as ochi (fall) or sage (lowering). Twelve kinds of ochi are codified and recognized. The earlier _kobanashi_ was a short comical vignette ending with an ochi. |
| [Plot twist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plot_twist "Plot twist") | Unexpected change ("twist") in the direction or expected outcome of the plot. See also [twist ending](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Twist_ending "Twist ending"). | An early example is the _[Arabian Nights](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arabian\_Nights "Arabian Nights")_ tale "[The Three Apples](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Three_Apples "The Three Apples")". A locked chest found by a fisherman contains a dead body, and two different men claim to be the murderer, which turns out to be the investigator's own slave. |
| [Poetic justice](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Poetic_justice "Poetic justice") | Virtue ultimately rewarded, or vice punished, by an ironic twist of fate related to the character's own conduct | [Wile E. Coyote](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wile_E._Coyote_and_the_Road_Runner "Wile E. Coyote and the Road Runner") coming up with a contraption to catch the Road Runner, only to be foiled and caught by his own devices. Each sin's punishment in [Dante's _Inferno_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inferno_(Dante) "Inferno (Dante)") is a symbolic instance of poetic justice. |
| [Predestination paradox](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Predestination_paradox "Predestination paradox") | Time travel paradox where a time traveler is caught in a loop of events that "predestines" them to travel back in time | In _[Doctor Who](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Doctor\_Who "Doctor Who")_, the main character repeatedly finds himself under the obligation of having to travel back in time because of something his future character has done. |
| [Red herring](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Red_herring "Red herring") | Diverting attention away from an item of significance. | For example, in mystery fiction, an innocent party may be purposefully cast as highly suspicious through emphasis or descriptive techniques to divert attention from the true guilty party. |
| [Self-fulfilling prophecy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Self-fulfilling_prophecy "Self-fulfilling prophecy") | Prediction that, by being made, makes itself come true. | Early examples include the legend of [Oedipus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oedipus "Oedipus"), and the story of [Krishna](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Krishna "Krishna") in the _[Mahabharata](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mahabharata "Mahabharata")_. There is also an example of this in _[Harry Potter](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harry\_Potter "Harry Potter")_ when Lord Voldemort heard a prophecy (made by Sybill Trelawney to Dumbledore) that a boy born at the end of July, whose parents had defied Voldemort thrice and survived, would be made marked as his equal. Because of this prophecy, Lord Voldemort sought out Harry Potter (believing him to be the boy spoken of) and tried to kill him. His parents died protecting him, and when Voldemort tried to cast a killing curse on Harry, it rebounded and took away most of his strength, and gave Harry Potter a unique ability and connection with the Dark Lord thus marking him as his equal. |
| _[Story within a story](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Story\_within\_a\_story "Story within a story")_ (Hypodiegesis) | A story told within another story. See also [frame story](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frame_story "Frame story"). | In Stephen King's _The Wind Through the Keyhole_, of the _Dark Tower_ series, the protagonist tells a story from his past to his companions, and in this story he tells another relatively unrelated story. |
| [Ticking time bomb scenario](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ticking_time_bomb_scenario "Ticking time bomb scenario") | Threat of impending disaster—often used in thrillers where salvation and escape are essential elements | In the [post-apocalyptic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nuclear_holocaust "Nuclear holocaust") novel _[On the Beach](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/On\_the\_Beach\_(novel) "On the Beach (novel)")_, the main characters face increasing [radioactivity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nuclear_fallout "Nuclear fallout") drifting [across the equator](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intertropical_Convergence_Zone "Intertropical Convergence Zone") toward Australia. Learning that the [worst](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Acute_radiation_syndrome "Acute radiation syndrome") is predicted to come sooner rather than later heightens the urgency and sense of immediacy felt by the characters and by the reader. |
| [Unreliable narrator](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unreliable_narrator "Unreliable narrator") | The narrator of the story is not sincere, or introduces a bias in their narration and possibly misleads the reader, hiding or minimizing events, characters, or motivations. | An example is _[The Murder of Roger Ackroyd](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The\_Murder\_of\_Roger\_Ackroyd "The Murder of Roger Ackroyd")_. The novel includes an unexpected [plot twist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plot_twist "Plot twist") at the end of the novel. In the last chapter, Sheppard describes how he was an [unreliable narrator](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unreliable_narrator "Unreliable narrator"). |

Perspective
-----------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=List_of_narrative_techniques&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Perspective")]

| Name | Definition | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Audience surrogate | A character who expresses the questions and confusion of the audience, with whom the audience can identify. Frequently used in detective fiction and science fiction, where the character asks a central character how they accomplished certain deeds, for the purpose of inciting that character to explain (for the curious audience) his or her methods, or a character asking a relatively educated person to explain what amounts to the backstory. | [Dr. Watson](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dr._Watson "Dr. Watson") in the Sherlock Holmes stories. Scott Evil, played by [Seth Green](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seth_Green "Seth Green"), son of Dr. Evil in the _[Austin Powers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Austin\_Powers "Austin Powers")_ films. The [companion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Companion_(Doctor_Who) "Companion (Doctor Who)") role in _[Doctor Who](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Doctor\_Who "Doctor Who")_, usually a contemporary human, giving the alien Doctor someone to explain situations to, for the benefit of the audience. [Dr. Jennifer Melfi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dr._Jennifer_Melfi "Dr. Jennifer Melfi") in _[The Sopranos](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The\_Sopranos "The Sopranos")_. |
| [Author surrogate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Author_surrogate "Author surrogate") | Characters which are based on authors, usually to support their personal views. Sometimes an intentionally or unintentionally idealized version of them. A variation is the [Mary Sue](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mary_Sue "Mary Sue") or Gary Stu, which primarily serves as an idealized self-insertion. | Socrates in the writings of Plato. Plato never speaks in his own voice in his dialogues. In the [_Second Letter_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second_Letter_(Plato) "Second Letter (Plato)"), it says, "no writing of Plato exists or ever will exist, but those now said to be his are those of a Socrates become beautiful and new". |
| Breaking the [fourth wall](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fourth_wall "Fourth wall") | An author or character addresses the audience directly (also known as direct address). This may acknowledge to the reader or audience that what is being presented is fiction, or may seek to extend the world of the story to provide the illusion that they are included in it. | The characters in _[Sesame Street](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sesame\_Street "Sesame Street")_ often break the fourth wall when they address their viewers as part of the ongoing storyline, which is possible because of the high level of [suspension of disbelief](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Suspension_of_disbelief "Suspension of disbelief") afforded by its audience—children. The English political drama show _[House of Cards](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/House\_of\_Cards\_(U.S.\_TV\_series) "House of Cards (U.S. TV series)")_ and its later American version, also use this technique frequently to let the viewers know what the main character [Frank Underwood](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frank_Underwood_(House_of_Cards) "Frank Underwood (House of Cards)") is thinking and planning. Ferris Bueller in _[Ferris Bueller's Day Off](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ferris\_Bueller%27s\_Day\_Off "Ferris Bueller's Day Off")_ frequently addresses the audience. |
| [Defamiliarization](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Defamiliarization "Defamiliarization") | Taking an everyday object and presenting it in a way that is weirdly unfamiliar so that we see the object in a new way. Coined by the early 20th-century Russian literary critic Viktor Shklovsk

--- SOURCE 4: Mastering Narrative Techniques - Number Analytics ---
URL: https://www.numberanalytics.com/blog/mastering-narrative-techniques

SUMMARY:
Explore the art of narrative technique in contemporary literature, from character development to plot twists and narrative voice.



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 5: Exploring Narrative Techniques in Contemporary Short Fiction ---
URL: https://artsreview.com.au/exploring-narrative-techniques-in-contemporary-short-fiction/

SUMMARY:
From non-linear narratives to unreliable narrators and fragmented structures, these methods offer fresh and engaging ways to experience stories.



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Investigating contemporary narrative techniques reveals how authors craft stories, employing tools like non-linear timelines and unreliable narrators to enhance engagement and emotional depth.
Examining reader engagement uncovers how narrative methods evoke emotional responses, prompting connections with characters and themes, ultimately enhancing the storytelling experience.

enriched_points.point_title: To explore implications for identity and memory
enriched_points.expanded_content: Analyzing narratives helps uncover how stories shape individual and collective identities, influencing perceptions of memory, cultural context, and personal experiences within contemporary literature.
